perturbation.original_text: Recipient agrees that it shall take reasonable measures to protect the secrecy of and avoid disclosure and unauthorized use of the Confidential Information. Without limiting the foregoing, Recipient shall take at least those measures that it takes to protect its own confidential information of a similar nature, but in no event less than reasonable measures.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient agrees that it shall endeavor to take measures to protect the secrecy of and avoid disclosure and unauthorized use of the Confidential Information. Recipient shall take measures to protect confidential information, which may include those measures that it takes to protect its own confidential information of a similar nature.
perturbation.explanation: The change from 'shall take reasonable measures' to 'shall endeavor to take measures' weakens the obligation. Removing the 'but in no event less than reasonable measures' creates further ambiguity. This makes the standard of care uncertain.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 3426.1 provides for injunctive relief for misappropriation of trade secrets. By weakening the obligation to protect confidential information, the 'endeavor to take measures' clause makes it more difficult to prove misappropriation and obtain injunctive relief, as it introduces a subjective standard of effort rather than a clear objective standard of protection.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to protect confidential information by changing 'shall take reasonable measures' to 'shall endeavor to take measures,' and removing the 'but in no event less than reasonable measures' clause. This creates ambiguity and a lower standard of care. The scraped law snippet defines 'Improper means' in California Civil Code § 3426.1 as including 'breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy'. By weakening the original obligation, the modified contract makes it harder to prove that a breach of duty occurred, potentially hindering the ability to obtain injunctive relief for misappropriation.

perturbation.original_text: At the request of the Discloser, Recipient will promptly return to the Discloser or at Discloser’s instructions destroy any written or electronic Confidential Information and all physical media on which Confidential Information was received from the other Recipient, including any copies thereof, with a letter confirming that the Confidential Information has not been reproduced or copied or that all copies have been returned and that none of the Confidential Information was disclosed to anyone except in accordance with this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: At the request of the Discloser, Recipient will use commercially reasonable efforts to return to the Discloser or at Discloser’s instructions destroy any written or electronic Confidential Information and all physical media on which Confidential Information was received from the other Recipient, including any copies thereof, with a letter confirming that, to the best of their knowledge, the Confidential Information has not been reproduced or copied or that all copies have been returned and that none of the Confidential Information was disclosed to anyone except in accordance with this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'promptly return' to 'use commercially reasonable efforts' weakens the obligation regarding the speed of return/destruction. Also, replacing the guarantee in the letter with 'to the best of their knowledge' weakens the assurance regarding non-disclosure and non-reproduction.
perturbation.law_explanation: 18 U.S. Code § 1836 protects trade secrets. Weakening the obligation to promptly return or destroy confidential information and weakening the assurance of non-disclosure increases the risk of trade secret misappropriation. The 'commercially reasonable efforts' standard introduces ambiguity and makes it harder to prove intentional misappropriation, as the recipient can argue they made reasonable efforts even if the information was compromised.

Civil proceedings (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.- (1) In general .-An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.- (A) In
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding the return or destruction of confidential information and the assurance of non-disclosure, potentially increasing the risk of trade secret misappropriation. Replacing "promptly return" with "commercially reasonable efforts" weakens the obligation, making it less stringent. Similarly, changing the guarantee to "to the best of their knowledge" reduces the assurance provided. 18 U.S. Code § 1836 allows for civil actions against misappropriation of trade secrets; weakening the return/destruction obligation could hinder proving intentional misappropriation, as the recipient could claim they made 'reasonable efforts' even if a breach occurred. The altered text creates a loophole, making enforcement more difficult, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Each Recipient acknowledges that a violation of this Agreement would cause irreparable harm to the Discloser for which no adequate remedy at law exists and each Recipient therefore agrees that, in addition to any other remedies available, both Parties will be entitled to seek injunctive relief to enforce the terms of this Agreement without a requirement that Discloser prove irreparable harm or provide the posting of a bond.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Recipient acknowledges that a violation of this Agreement could potentially cause harm to the Discloser, and each Recipient therefore agrees that, in addition to any other remedies available, both Parties may consider seeking injunctive relief to enforce the terms of this Agreement, subject to applicable legal standards and requirements.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'would cause irreparable harm' to 'could potentially cause harm' and 'will be entitled to seek' to 'may consider seeking' makes seeking injunctive relief discretionary. Also, adding 'subject to applicable legal standards and requirements' reintroduces the requirement to prove irreparable harm.
perturbation.law_explanation: Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 65 governs injunctions and temporary restraining orders. While the original text explicitly waives the requirement to prove irreparable harm, the modified text introduces ambiguity and potentially reinstates the requirement to prove irreparable harm under FRCP 65, contradicting the original intent of the agreement to expedite injunctive relief.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 65. Injunctions and Restraining Orders | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 65. Injunctions and Restraining Orders (a) Preliminary Injunction. (1) Notice. The court may issue a preliminary injunction only on notice to the adverse party. (2) Consolidating the Hearing with the Trial on the Merits. Before or after beginning the hearing on a motion for a preliminary injunction, the court may advance the trial on the merits and consolidate it with the hearing. Even when consolidation is not ordered, evidence that is received on the motion and that would be admissible at trial becomes part of the trial record and need not be repeated at trial. But the court must preserve any party's right to a jury trial. (b) Temporary Restraining Order. (1) Issuing Without Notice. The court may issue a temporary restraining order without written or oral notice to the adverse party or its attorney only if: (A) specific facts in an affidavit or a verified complaint clearly show that immediate and irreparable injury, loss, or damage will result to the movant before the adverse party can be heard in opposition; and (B) the movant's attorney certifies in writing any efforts made to give notice and the reasons why it should not be required. (2) Contents; Expiration. Every temporary restraining order issued without notice must state the date and hour it was issued; describe the injury and state why it is irreparable; state why the order was issued without notice; and be promptly filed in the clerk's office and entered in the record. The order expires at the time after entry—not to exceed 14 days—that the court sets, unless before that time the court, for good cause, extends it for a like period or the adverse party consents to a longer extension. The reasons for an extension must be entered in the record. (3) Expediting the Preliminary-Injunction Hearing. If the order is issued without notice, the moti

Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding the requirement to prove irreparable harm for injunctive relief, potentially contradicting the original intent of waiving this requirement. The original text states that each recipient acknowledges that a violation 'would cause irreparable harm,' while the changed text softens this to 'could potentially cause harm'. Additionally, the original states both parties 'will be entitled to seek injunctive relief,' while the modified text states they 'may consider seeking' injunctive relief, which is less definitive. By adding 'subject to applicable legal standards and requirements,' the modified text reintroduces the need to demonstrate irreparable harm, as required by FRCP 65, effectively contradicting the initial waiver. Scraped Law Snippet 1 discusses injunctions and restraining orders under Rule 65, implicitly highlighting the procedural requirements that the original text sought to bypass, and that the changed text now potentially subjects the parties to.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by, and construed in accordance with, the laws of the State of ____________________ without giving effect to any conflicts or choice of laws provisions that would cause the application of the domestic substantive laws of any other jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be interpreted in a manner generally consistent with the principles of law.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing the specific state law with 'principles of law' introduces significant ambiguity. It is no longer clear which jurisdiction's law governs the agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware Code Title 6, § 2708 allows parties to a contract to choose Delaware law to govern the contract, even if the parties, the agreement, or the transaction does not bear a reasonable relation to Delaware. The modified text, by removing the specific choice of law (i.e. selecting a state), makes the contractual intentions regarding choice of law unclear, possibly invalidating the selection of Delaware law and thus contradicting the principles of the law.

perturbation.justification: The original text specifies that the agreement is governed by the laws of a particular state, providing clarity and legal certainty. The changed text replaces this with a general reference to 'principles of law,' introducing ambiguity and making it unclear which jurisdiction's law applies. This directly undermines the ability to determine the governing law, which is a fundamental aspect of contract enforceability, and increases the risk of disputes over interpretation. The Law Explanation further supports this by stating that removing the specific choice of law makes the contractual intentions regarding choice of law unclear, possibly invalidating the selection of Delaware law and thus contradicting the principles of the law.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties expressly agree and acknowledge that any disputes relating to this Agreement will be exclusively adjudicated by federal and state courts located in_______________________.
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties agree that disputes relating to this Agreement may be adjudicated in courts.
perturbation.explanation: The term 'may be adjudicated' removes the certainty, exclusivity, and location of the forum for dispute resolution. This introduces ambiguity.
perturbation.law_explanation: 28 U.S.C. § 1391 governs venue in federal courts. By removing the exclusive forum selection clause, the parties may be forced to litigate in a venue that is inconvenient or strategically disadvantageous, potentially contradicting the original intent of the agreement to have disputes resolved in a specific location. The amended clause allows for arguments that jurisdiction is appropriate elsewhere, conflicting with the purpose of a forum selection clause to provide certainty and predictability.

Venue generally (a) Applicability of Section .-Except as otherwise provided by law- (1) this section shall govern the venue of all civil actions brought in district courts of the United States; and (2) the proper venue for a civil action shall be determined without regard to whether the action is local or transitory in nature. perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 28 U.S. Code § 1391 - Venue generally | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 28 U.S. Code § 1391 - Venue generally U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) Applicability of Section.— Except as otherwise provided by law— (1) this section shall govern the venue of all civil actions brought in district courts of the United States; and (2) the proper venue for a civil action shall be determined without regard to whether the action is local or transitory in nature. (b) Venue in General.— A civil action may be brought in— (1) a judicial district in which any defendant resides, if all defendants are residents of the State in which the district is located; (2) a judicial district in which a substantial part of the events or omissions giving rise to the claim occurred, or a substantial part of property that is the subject of the action is situated; or (3) if there is no district in which an action may otherwise be brought as provided in this section, any judicial district in which any defendant is subject to the court’s personal jurisdiction with respect to such action. (c) Residency.— For all venue purposes— (1) a natural person, including an alien lawfully admitted for permanent residence in the United States, shall be deemed to reside in the judicial district in which that person is domiciled; (2) an entity with the capacity to sue and be sued in its common name under applicable law, whether or not incorporated, shall be deemed to reside, if a defendant, in any judicial district in which such defendant is subject to the court’s personal jurisdiction with respect to the civil action in question and, if a plaintiff, only in the judicial district in which it maintains its principal place of business; and (3) a defendant not resident in the United States may be sued in any judicial district, and the joinder of such a defendant shall be disregarded in determining where the action may be brought w
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies exclusive adjudication in a particular location. The changed text, stating disputes 'may be adjudicated in courts,' removes this exclusivity and location, introducing ambiguity. This contradicts the purpose of a forum selection clause, which, as the Law Explanation states, is 'to provide certainty and predictability.' While 28 U.S.C. § 1391, as seen in both scraped snippets, governs venue generally in federal courts, it does not negate the right of parties to agree on a specific venue through a forum selection clause. By making venue discretionary, the changed text weakens the legally binding nature of such agreements, rendering the venue uncertain.
